title: Cost-of-living crunch bites
full_text: Other Editions 06 May 2026 Business Cost-of-living crunch bites The Australian Perry Williams, David Ross, Additional reporting: Max Aitchison, Tansy Harcourt 6 May 2026 973 words English AUSTLN 15 © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. Top retailers say households are being forced to cut spending as a third consecutive interest rate rise and a spike in fuel costs triggered by the Iran war worsens a cost-of-living crunch across the nation’s economy. Financial stress among households has increased in the past few months, and consumer confidence has fallen amid rising inflation and high mortgage bills, Wesfarmers managing director Rob Scott warned. “Clearly for low-income families that are struggling to make ends meet, they are simply spending less money than they were a couple of months ago,” Mr Scott told the Macquarie Australia conference in Sydney on Tuesday. “Fuel prices are going up, other costs are going up and interest rates are going up.” Westpac chief executive Anthony Miller said the response from some cash-strapped households had not been surprising given the uncertain economic conditions. “Not surprisingly overall, consumer spending has slowed since the [Iran] conflict began reflecting pressure from higher interest rates and the expiry of electricity rebates,” Mr Miller said on Tuesday. The Reserve Bank on Tuesday lifted the target cash rate to 4.35 per cent, now equal to the highest interest rate in the inflation surge after the Covid crisis, and piling further pressure on the government to respond with cost-of-living measures in next week’s federal budget. Endeavour Group – the owner of Dan Murphy’s liquor stores and a network of 350 national pubs – said households pulled back on spending at its pubs in March and April as interest rate rises and inflation slow momentum in the economy. “March was a big change from a retail standpoint. The hotels market went backwards quite significantly in March for alcoholic beverages and in hotels we could see quite a changed dynamic,” Endeavour CEO Jayne Hrdlicka told the conference. “And you can see that consumers are feeling the cost-of-living pressure. “They’re feeling the impact of mortgages. The cost of living has gone up as a consequence of increased interest costs and mort­gages and the increased cost of fuel, and that’s starting to flow through to inflation.” The Australian reported this week economic ructions will force the Albanese government to announce a major multibillion-dollar cash splash for all working Australians as its centrepiece cost-of-living support in the May budget, adding pressure to the Reserve Bank’s fight against inflation and interest rate decisions. Wesfarmers – the owner of Bunnings, Kmart and Officeworks – maintained that the overall economy was resilient. “Despite some of the pressures that we’re working through at the moment, Australia and the Australian economy is in reasonably good shape,” Mr Scott said. However, he said Australia now faced significant pressure to increase wages as the Iran war stoked high inflation across the economy. “You can expect that there is going to be a lot of pressure on wages as a result of what we’re seeing play out with inflation,” Mr Scott said. “The reality is inflation flows through the market in many ways. It’s an unstoppable trend. It’s a very challenging issue. “So everyone in Australia is going to be impacted by inflation.” Wesfarmers said it was juggling a raft of cost increases across fuel surcharges, container shipping, domestic transport operators, petrochemicals and building products inflation. “We are seeing a fair bit of cost pressure flowing through these products,” Mr Scott said. Endeavour said the Iran war and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz shipping lane had forced every Australian business to review its exposure to any immediate and also medium-term supply chain snags. “This is unprecedented. The dynamic in front of us, and so most CEOs around the country would be doing exactly what we did, which is step back once the Strait was closed and the knock-on consequences you start to work your way through,” Ms Hrdlicka said. “What we’ve done now is to say a handful of days’ inventory is fine in normal circumstances. But we don’t actually have the ability to see through what the future circumstances look like, and until we do we probably need to have a bit more resilience, and we need more days of inventory in our system. We can’t see around the corner.” Qantas CEO Vanessa Hudson said the ongoing cost-of-living crisis was not affecting travel in the same way it was forcing some consumers to cut back on discretionary spending such as alcohol and entertainment. Ms Hudson told the conference that intention to travel had “remained very positive both domestically and internationally”. “Travel has remained number one on the discretionary expenditure priority, and domestic travel is number two. That has remained the same for the last at least five years,” Ms Hudson said. Industrial conglomerate SGH’s chief executive, Ryan Stokes, said there was still a lot of underlying structural pressure on inflation. But he said infrastructure remained relatively positive. “There’s been project delays, but overall it still feels relatively strong right now,” Mr Stokes said. “Twelve months from now I’m not quite sure how that will roll out, but there is still the demand driver from a residential construction perspective.” ROB SCOTT WESFARMERS CEO There is going to be a lot of pressure on wages as a result of what we’re seeing play out with inflation JAYNE HRDLICKA ENDEAVOUR GROUP CEO You can see that consumers are feeling the cost of living pressure. summary: Australia is experiencing a significant cost-of-living crisis, exacerbated by a third consecutive interest rate rise and rising fuel costs due to the Iran war. Major retailers report decreased consumer spending, particularly among low-income households, while the Reserve Bank's latest cash rate increase puts further pressure on inflation. The Albanese government is expected to announce substantial cost-of-living support in the upcoming federal budget.
publication:
Aussies reject AI – but it’s not for the reason you think

Chief executives of Australia’s biggest companies and Anthony Albanese want us all to use more artificial intelligence-powered tools – but there is a big problem.
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Consumer spending has slowed as households face rising interest rates and inflation.
The Reserve Bank raised the cash rate to 4.35%, the highest since the post-Covid inflation surge.
Major companies are adjusting inventory strategies due to supply chain uncertainties linked to the Iran conflict.
relevance: Understanding the economic pressures on households is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals in assessing risk and developing financial products.
